description: Detect logical fallacies — errors in reasoning that undermine the validity of an argument, whether deployed deliberately or carelessly.

rules.name: Straw Man
rules.explanation: Misrepresents or exaggerates an opponent's position to make it easier to attack. The real argument is replaced with a distorted version. Look for paraphrases that insert extremes, absolutes, or implications the original didn't contain, followed by a rebuttal of the distortion.

rules.name: Ad Hominem
rules.explanation: Attacks the person making the argument rather than the argument itself. The validity of a claim is independent of who states it. Look for character attacks, credential questioning, or motive impugning that substitutes for addressing the actual point.

rules.name: Appeal to Authority (Unsubstantiated)
rules.explanation: Cites unnamed or unverifiable authority as proof. Legitimate appeal to authority names specific sources with verifiable credentials in the relevant field. Red flags: no names, no citations, no publication, vague collective nouns ('experts', 'scientists', 'researchers'), or the authority cited is outside their domain of expertise.

rules.name: Appeal to Emotion
rules.explanation: Substitutes emotional reaction (fear, pity, outrage, disgust) for logical reasoning. The emotional charge is used to bypass critical evaluation. A valid argument can include emotion, but here the emotion IS the argument — remove it and no reasoning remains.

rules.name: Appeal to Popularity (Bandwagon)
rules.explanation: Argues a claim is true because many people believe it. Popularity has no bearing on truth — millions believed the earth was flat. Look for head-count language that substitutes for evidence: quantifiers about how many agree, combined with an absence of actual proof.

rules.name: Appeal to Nature
rules.explanation: Equates 'natural' with 'good' or 'unnatural' with 'bad'. Arsenic is natural; insulin injections are unnatural. The naturalistic fallacy conflates 'is' with 'ought'. Look for the word 'natural' used as a value judgment rather than a factual descriptor.

rules.name: Appeal to Tradition
patterns: we've always done it this way
the way things have always been
it's been this way for centuries
rules.explanation: Argues something is correct because it has been done that way for a long time. Duration of practice has no bearing on validity. Look for temporal longevity used as the primary justification — the past substituting for evidence.

rules.name: Appeal to Ignorance
patterns: hasn't been shown to be false
rules.explanation: Argues a claim is true because it hasn't been proven false, or vice versa. The burden of proof lies with the claimant. Lack of disproof is not proof. Look for demands to prove a negative — shifting the burden of proof to the opponent.

rules.name: False Dilemma / False Dichotomy
patterns: either you're with us or against us
rules.explanation: Presents only two options when more exist. Eliminates nuance, compromise, and alternative solutions. Look for binary framing ('either/or', 'only two choices') especially on complex issues where a spectrum of positions is possible. The missing options are often the most reasonable ones.

rules.name: Slippery Slope
rules.explanation: Argues that one action will trigger an unstoppable chain of increasingly extreme consequences without establishing the causal links between each step. Each step in the chain requires its own evidence. Look for catastrophic chains where the jumps between steps are asserted not proven.

rules.name: False Cause (Post Hoc / Correlation)
patterns: the data clearly shows a link
rules.explanation: Assumes that because B followed A, A caused B (post hoc ergo propter hoc), or that correlation implies causation. Temporal sequence and statistical correlation do not establish causal mechanisms. Look for causal claims without mechanistic explanation, controlled studies, or elimination of confounding variables.

rules.name: Hasty Generalization
rules.explanation: Draws a broad conclusion from a small, unrepresentative sample. Personal anecdotes, single examples, or cherry-picked cases are extrapolated to entire populations. Look for universal quantifiers ('all', 'every', 'always', 'never') supported by only one or two examples.

rules.name: Circular Reasoning / Begging the Question
patterns: the evidence is the claim itself
rules.explanation: The conclusion is assumed in the premise. The argument goes in a circle — A is true because of B, and B is true because of A. Look for conclusions that merely restate the premise in different words, or chains of reasoning where the starting point and endpoint are the same claim.

rules.name: Tu Quoque (Whataboutism)
rules.explanation: Deflects criticism by pointing at the accuser's behavior. Whether the accuser is a hypocrite is irrelevant to the truth of their claim. A thief who says 'stealing is wrong' is correct regardless of their own behavior. Look for counter-accusations that change the subject rather than address the argument.

rules.name: No True Scotsman
rules.explanation: When confronted with a counterexample, redefines the group to exclude it. 'No Scotsman would do that.' 'But McTavish did.' 'Well, no TRUE Scotsman would.' The definition shifts to become unfalsifiable. Look for ad-hoc redefinition of categories after a counterexample is presented.

rules.name: Red Herring
patterns: what we should really be talking about
rules.explanation: Introduces an irrelevant topic to divert attention from the original argument. The diversion may be interesting or emotionally compelling but does not address the point being debated. Look for topic shifts that abandon the original question without resolving it, often using transitional phrases that bridge to unrelated territory.

rules.name: Loaded Question
rules.explanation: Asks a question that presupposes something unproven. Any direct answer confirms the embedded assumption. 'Have you stopped cheating?' presupposes cheating occurred. Look for questions where answering 'yes' or 'no' both concede a contested premise.

rules.name: Equivocation
patterns: it depends on what you mean by
that's one way to look at it
rules.explanation: Uses the same word with different meanings at different points in the argument, making the conclusion appear to follow when it doesn't. The semantic drift is often subtle — look for key terms that shift from a common usage to a technical one (or vice versa) within the same passage.

rules.name: Moving the Goalposts
rules.explanation: After evidence meets the original demand, the criteria are changed so the evidence is never sufficient. The finish line moves every time you approach it. Look for escalating demands — each time one requirement is met, a new one appears that wasn't mentioned before.

rules.name: Special Pleading
rules.explanation: Claims an exemption from a generally accepted rule without adequate justification. The standard applies to everyone except the speaker's favoured case — for reasons that amount to 'because I said so'. Look for unjustified exception claims, especially when the reasoning for the exception isn't substantially different from cases where the rule is applied.

rules.name: Genetic Fallacy
patterns: the source alone should tell you
rules.explanation: Judges a claim by its origin rather than its current merit. A good idea from a bad source is still a good idea. A medicine discovered by accident is no less effective. Look for arguments where the origin, history, or creator of something is presented as the sole basis for accepting or rejecting it.

rules.name: Composition / Division
patterns: if one of them is then all
rules.explanation: Composition: assumes what is true of the parts must be true of the whole (every player is great, therefore the team is great). Division: assumes what is true of the whole must be true of every part (the team won, so every player performed well). Look for unsupported part-to-whole or whole-to-part transfers.

rules.name: False Equivalence / Category Error
patterns: one is just as bad as the other
rules.explanation: Treats fundamentally different things as if they are the same, or equates two situations that differ in critical ways. The speaker flattens important distinctions — scale, context, intent, severity — to force a false parallel. Look for direct equivalence claims between things that share superficial similarity but differ in substance, or 'both sides' framing that erases meaningful asymmetry.
